A country where a construction worker earns $4,000 for 160 hours of work, while a teacher or a doctor earns only... $500–1,000.

This says a lot about what the authorities truly value: not educated, independent-minded people who can distinguish between right and wrong, but obedient workers who will support any system of power without questioning it.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
In the video, we wanted to show the culture, people, nature, history, and daily life, not ignore the political reality or travel risks.
You are right that visitors should always check current travel advisories and understand the situation before going anywhere. Safety can feel very different depending on nationality, timing, and personal experience. I appreciate you adding this perspective because it helps other viewers think more carefully too.

replies.text: It’s wonderful to hear this from someone who has visited Belarus several times. The cleanliness, kind people, and good food are exactly the kind of details that make a country feel special. The language barrier can be a challenge, but kindness often speaks louder than words. What was your favorite food or place in Minsk during your visits? ❤️

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries

comments.author: @carsten9168-hs4gs
comments.text: Malaysian here. Problem is Belarus has 9 months of cold, winter weather like Russia. Then we have dictator Lukashenko ruling since 1992 with virtually no dissent and trouble if you crossed the line. When democracy is brought back, that's the day tourists will flock. Belarus had a terrible history with 850 villages all burnt by Nazi occupation especially the Babruisk massacres. Belarus is blessed with beautiful, elegant women like Ukraine with emphasis on education, classical arts and family life all deeply rooted in tradition !

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
You are right, Belarus has a very complex history and political reality, and those things should never be ignored. The suffering during World War II, especially the burned villages and massacres, left a deep mark on the country and its people.
At the same time, Belarus also has a quiet beauty, strong traditions, educated people, and a culture that deserves to be understood beyond politics. Hopefully one day, when the situation becomes more open, more travelers will feel safe and welcome to discover it.

Such beauty descended from destruction. Seems a common theme in the Universe.

Why or why not?
